text.fullText: From: stephaniejhemphill@everyactioncustom.com To: OW-Docket <OW-Docket@epa.gov> Date: 2/10/2026 11:27:08 AM Subject: Docket ID# EPA–HQ–OW–2025–2929 — Opposition to Proposed 2026 Changes to Circumscribe Certification Authority of States and Tribes -------------------------------------------------------------------------- ---------------------------------------------------------------- [You don't often get email from stephaniejhemphill@everyactioncustom.com. Learn why this is important at https://aka.ms/LearnAboutSenderIdentification ] Caution: This email originated from outside EPA, please exercise additional caution when deciding whether to open attachments or click on provided links. Dear Administrator Lee Zeldin, I wish to communicate my strong opposition to the EPA’s proposed 2026 rule changes that would limit certification authority used by States and Tribes to protect clean water. The Clean Water Act was adopted by Congress to restore and maintain clean water and to ensure that States and Tribes could prevent violation of the water quality requirements they have adopted. These proposed certification rules would serve the special interests of polluters at the expense of clean water, the rights of States to protect the health and welfare of their residents, and the sovereignty of Tribes. 1. ) Near me, the Fond du Lac Band of Lake Superior Chippewa has done pioneering and effective work to protect the water quality in the St. Louis River. They rely on federal support, and the federal government has a trust responsibility to help them. Therefore, I oppose the Rule’s elimination of current regulations facilitating Tribal certification authority and supporting neighboring/downstream Tribes in objecting to federal permits and preventing violations of Reservation water quality requirements. 2. ) In Minnesota, we’ve used existing certification authority to prevent degradation and protect streams, Exceptional Aquatic Life Use waters, and Outstanding Resource Value waters, like the Boundary Waters Canoe Area Wilderness and Voyageurs National Park. Therefore, I oppose the Rule’s restrictions that would prevent any State or Tribe in the United States from setting conditions on Federal “general permits.” 3. ) It is a mistake to tighten a noose around the scope of certification. The proposed rules would prevent conditions or denial of Federal permits even if activities would violate State and Tribal standards and contaminate lakes and streams due to wetlands destruction, waste stockpiling, or seepage through groundwater into surface water. We have a lot of experience with this in the Minnesota mining country. 4. ) I oppose the Rule’s forced waiver of State or Tribal certification after a year even if time is needed due to public process, acts of God, or because the project proposer concealed the truth about water impacts in its draft permit application. This rule would encourage polluters to hide the facts and do a bait-and-switch after time has run out for certification. 5. ) I oppose the Rule’s creation of new power in the EPA Administrator to determine without guardrails that an entire category of discharges will never affect downstream States or Tribes and deprive them of authority to object to violations of water quality requirements. What category would this EPA remove from certification review? All pipeline discharge? All mining waste? The EPA must reject or withdraw its 2026 proposed rule to undermine State and Tribal certification. Existing rules under the Clean Water Act do a much better job of protecting us and our children from contaminated water that they can’t safely drink, swim in, or fish. Thank you for considering my comments, Sincerely, Stephanie Hemphill 3614 Prindle Rd. Duluth MN 55803 Sincerely, Stephanie Hemphill 3614 Prindle Rd Duluth, MN 55803-9340 stephaniejhemphill@outlook.com
